1 5. On information and belief, LES GOTTESMAN is a natural person doing |

3 defendant GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY in the capacity of Humanities and English
sg Department Chairperson.
6. Defendants DOES ONE through TWENTY, inclusive, are individuals who
. 5 are responsible for the acts and omissions alleged below. Dr. Balla is presently
6 unaware of the identities of DOES ONE through TWENTY, inclusive, and therefore |
7 sues them with fictitious names. | | !
8 7. All acts and omissions alleged herein substantively occurred in the State of
, 9 California, in the County of San Francisco. Venue and jurisdiction are therefore
” proper in this forum.
iN 8. Dr. Balla began working for defendant Golden Gate University in and
around September, 2001, as an English tutor at the graduate and undergraduate level.
12 Dr. Balla served in this capacity regularly, with excellence and without incident, well
13 into his seventh, or what university tradition construes as sabbatical year.
| 14 9. Dr. Balla has a Ph. D. (University of Michigan, 1977), and has worked
15 professionally, and with distinction, for over 30 years, in at least five countries, in
- 16 human rights, and in the fields of writing, editing, and teaching.
71 10. While representing the United States as first Fulbright lecturer at the
farthest-eastern university in Czechoslovakia — before, during, and after the 1989 fall
18 of communism there — Dr. Balla saw how higher education in eastern Europe did not
19 challenge, but reinforced the deeper hatreds and misunderstandings of latent -
. 20 jl. nationalisms there: 1) stressing specializations in university departments only isolated
21 from each other; 2) promoting the veneers of impersonality; and 3) enforcing the
» platitudes of Marxism-Leninism as cultural commonality. Dr. Balla proceeded to
23 obtain foundation monies and began what was central and eastern Europe’s first-ever
. program to rotate guest intellectuals from the neighboring region before student
24 . audiences to explore personal and cultural connections: “Other Cultures — Inner
25 Values,” at the main university in Budapest, Hungary, 1991-92.
26 11. From these experiences, Dr. Balla saw how literacy may better engage the
"97 personal and the cultural, his program hereafter called Essaying Differences. The
28

 
1 “Proprietor’s Column” at wivw, EssayingDifferences.com shows his evolving | |
7 2 understanding of how American universities, too, reduce literacy — especially reducing |
3 bridges between the personal and the cultural. The reigning conceits of the former |
communist states all pale before our own corporate academe’s orthodoxies for 1)
| 4 departments separating by mutually-isolated specializations; 2) instructors and
5 students alike learning to equate “professionalism” with impersonality conceits; and 3) ;
6 administrators fitting the same entitlement culture inside universities as Americans |
7 practice outside as entitlement consumerism.
8 12. For these years’-long experiences, and as shown in his several dozen
9 “Proprietor’s Column” essays, Dr. Balla alleges FACT ONE: that he was well- |
10 prepared to teach the fall 2007 course at defendant Golden Gate University, and well-
knew the challenges students would feel: for that nexus of literacy, personal
MN involvement, and cultural reference.
2 13. Following his unblemished track record going into his seventh, or
13 sabbatical year, tutoring with defendant Golden Gate University, Dr. Balla was hired
14 by defendant Golden Gate University in mid-summer 2007 to teach Graduate Writing,
15 English 301, for the fall 2007 semester. First approached for this, he stressed |
16 immediately to the Department Chair, defendant Les Gottesman, that his course would
; have students acknowledging, quoting, and otherwise referring to each other — an
"7 introduction of personal and cultural linkages otherwise unknown in the corporate
18 | academe whose culture students have long seen only as relentlessly impersonal in all
19 its sequestered departments.
- 20 |, 14. Well prior to the commencement of the fall 2007 English 301 course, Dr.
21 | Balla submitted his first draft of his Course Syllabus to the Department Chair,
» defendant Les Gottesman. As tutor at defendant Golden Gate University, Dr. Balla
33 had seen dozens of students taking this English graduate writing course from various
instructors for going on seven years, so he well knew its parameters. Still, before
24 ; finishing his own first draft of his proposed syllabus, he carefully reviewed sample
25 syllabi from this course’s previous instructors.
26 |
99 .
28

 
1 15. Dr. Balla also sent a copy of his first draft syllabus to the immediately |
2 previous instructor, Carol Jameson (no longer affiliated with Golden Gate University,
; but experienced in having taught English 301 for many years).
4 16. Dr. Balla then accommodated the suggestions for revisions — none
tendered from Dr. Jameson, those from Chair Gottesman all seeking more student
- > happiness. Dr. Balla then submitted his final Course Syllabus by e-mail to the
6 appropriate offices at defendant Golden Gate University, and delivered a paper copy to
7 the office of defendant Les Gottesman. As FACT ONE remained true — that Dr. Balla |
8 was eminently prepared to modify the course for literacy’s challenges linking the
9 personal and the cultural — and as his proposed course well honored the standards of
7 | those before it, Course Syllabus was accepted without further revision, well before the
course began.
Mu 17. After teaching the English 301 class for approximately one month, Dr. |
I2 Balla was relieved from his teaching duties on October 5, 2007 by defendant Les |
13 Gottesman for ambiguous reason. In his e-mail of October 5, 2007, Chair Gottesman !
14 cited only “circumstances” and tacitly admitted that he was “still gathering |
15 information” in relation to the substance of the allegations against Dr. Balla. :
16 18. Dr. Balla thereafter made several verbal and written requests to be
7 informed of the exact allegations and to address the charges in a cordial environment
with the defendant Golden Gate University’s staff.
18 19. On November 28, 2007 Dr. Balla was informed via a letter from defendant
19 Terri Schultis, Director of Human Resources, that Plaintiff had been accused of:
. 20 ||. a. Allegation 1): Dr. Balla made derogatory remarks about one or more of
21 the students’ ethnic background, thereby creating a hostile environment.
» b. Allegation 2): Dr. Balla made specific remarks of a sexual nature,
23 regarding masturbation.
; c. Allegation 3): Dr. Balla invited students to his home in such a way as to
24 give the appearance that Dr. Balla’s motives included establishing a
25 romantic relationship with the students.
26 || 20. Asto Allegation 1), while the November 28, 2007 letter claimed
97 “sufficient evidence” that it was “likely” Plaintiff had made derogatory and
28

 
1 unacceptable remarks to students; specificity as to the evidence was withheld. As to
. 2 the charge of discrimination in Allegation 1), the November 28, 2007 letter cited
3 insufficient evidence to support a claim of discrimination.
4 21. The only statements Plaintiff made in reference to Allegation 1) regarding
“derogatory” and “unacceptable comments” were items read by the students from the
5 Course Syllabus’s three authors, George Orwell, W. H. Auden, and A. J. Liebling, and
6 from the students’ writings and oral comments on their own cultural backgrounds.
7 22. As to Allegation 2), Dr. Balla on information and belief asserts that his
8 English 301 students confused the word “emasculating” from the first paragraph of
, 9 ; one of the three assigned readings with the very-different word “masturbation.” ;
10 23. Dr. Balla further asserts, as to Allegation 3), that he offered further
iM assistance outside the classroom — either in the Writing Center of defendant Golden
Gate University, where he regularly provided in-person tutoring, going on seven years,
12 or at his home, for one or two students at a time as any liked. Dr. Balla lives at his
iB home with a long-term partner, Sue Noda, and over the years often had students over
14 if any needed extra help. This offer stemmed from Plaintiff's love of and dedication
15 to teaching, and had no sexual innuendo ever.
164, 24. The defendants at Golden Gate University never at any time showed
WI: interest in or were willing to discuss the ways the Course Syllabus challenged students
into the legitimate vulnerability that good teachers often use to key advancement. On
18 information and belief, defendants instead only made inquiries, requested, solicited,
19 and made statements, both verbal and in writing, to and from faculty and students
. 20 alike, advancing baseless hysteria. Their groveling in emotionalism only, completely
21 severed from all pedagogic context, only trumped a university’s proper educational .
7 mission. These Golden Gate University defendants cared not at all for the educational
3 challenges the students had with stirred emotions already embraced, but aimed instead
. only at creating a written record inflating the aforementioned unsubstantiated
24 ; allegations leveled against Dr. Balla.
25 |. 25. Requesting a meeting with defendant Barbara Karlin, Dr. Balla on
26 December 5, 2007 received a letter from her admitting the baseless nature of the
27 charges that defendant Golden Gate University had indulged, she writing, “To recap,
28 |
. |
; |

 
1 the finding of the inquiry indicated that the alleged behaviors did not constitute sexual —
7 7) harassment or discrimination.” |
3 26. Defendant Karlin, like the others, however, chose to indulge her own
personal bent for perceived miscreant emotions, and to stroke these totally out of the
4 approved educational context that stirred them. In her December 5 letter she stressed
: 5 her and the other defendants’ fear that the students had a “stated intention of all
; 6 dropping the course” — oblivious to the fact that, for dropping any course, students at
7 defendant Golden Gate University never had to present any complaints. |
8 27. The defendants’ repeatedly stressing their fear that the students had a
, 9 I “stated intention of all dropping the course” should invite attention to the fact that the
10 students were so indeed stirred and challenged, even as they were unprepared
emotionally or in literacy skills to handle these new challenges. The students went to
Ny administrators not to drop the course, but in evidence of FACT TWO: that they were
12 engaged in the challenges newly stirred in them exactly as they were just beginning to
123 see how they lacked the resources to handle them. Dr. Balla, as first party in this .
| 14 action, had begun what he had planned — and as FACT ONE shows him to have been
15 well qualified to set in motion. The students, as second party in this action, well
- 164 deserved to learn the literacy to deal with these challenges, as the Course Syllabus
| expressly set. The administrators, as third party in this action, should have respected
" the students’ right to learn, and the instructor’s contracted role to teach.
18 : 28. As part of defendant Golden Gate University’s indulging emotions apart
19 | from educational context, defendant Golden Gate University at all stages ignored the
: 20 . syllabus-contracted forays into literacy well underway. In its indulgence instead only
21 | in emotions, and ever out of pedagogic context, Dr. Balla was not provided access to
- 9 any of the aforementioned unsubstantiated allegations. He was not formally informed
93 of the aforementioned unsubstantiated damaging allegations in writing until after a
decision by the defendants had been rendered.
24 * 29, The aforementioned unsubstantiated damaging allegations and statements
25 | by defendants, written and oral, are materially false and have caused substantial
26 | damage to Dr. Balla’s professional standing in the community, and have brought
“27 tangible economic damages.
28

 
1 30. Asa result of the aforementioned written and oral false statements by
2 defendants, Dr. Balla has lost at least $38,000 in income and suffered irreparable °
3 damages to his unblemished reputation as an educational professional. —
4 31. As a result of being subjected to the aforementioned written and oral false
statements, Dr. Balla has suffered emotional distress, a diminished professional
5 reputation, a diminished community reputation, and other general damages in an
6 amount to be proven at trial.
7 32. Asa result of defendants’ failure to honor contracted pedagogic context, |
8 Dr. Balla alleges, finally, FACT THREE: that defendants endanger all of us by their
9 | reduction of university life to the “ethics” of pleasing consumers. Our larger
1 0 consumerism may be charming at many levels, just as its advertising channels may
M please for its many levels of promises, but Dr. Balla further asserts that we are not
simply consumers, that our identities need not come so disproportionately from
2 marketers, or from academics who for their corporate priorities would set all to their
_ iB cults of impersonality. FACT THREE opposes those who so cater to human fears
14 only further to gird the impersonality conceits key to their mutually-isolated
15 specializations and entitlement consumerism. Dr. Balla notes that our founding
- 6. fathers designed a successful republic not by anathematizing the personal and
i
7K: fetishizing the impersonal, but by carefully looking at all ranges of actual personality
throughout human history, and being sure to found a republic out of respect for the
18 3 actual mix of character, values, and other human tendencies. Our jurisprudence since
19 then has a historic legacy for valuing the pragmatic and the actually human against the
; 20 jl. many variously other tendencies to abridge these freedoms. Dr. Balla further notes
21 | how, as a Vietnamese translator-interpreter himself in the U.S. Army four decades
2 ago, he saw firsthand what happens when we impose our conceits onto peoples and
23 cultures whom we cannot see for the very biases and blindness built into our
bureaucratic and other corporate-based priorities. Dr. Balla finally notes that his
24 course well honored the larger freedoms of larger, wider literacy — via the contracted
25 ||. syllabus authors Orwell, Auden, and Liebling, and actual students learning to see and
26 | quote each other from within and among their own self-professed cultures.
